text: The advice goes back as far as Aristotle: "The only way to learn to play the flute is to play the flute."

author: @brianfisher6799
text: Anyone ever try using a brake rotor as the "base" of a wind generator?  Was replacing brake rotors today and all the r Murray Smith wind generator videos i watched made me stop and think, wow this is really round and has 5 bolts already attached and can be had for cheap, it is a bit heavy but could this be a component of a wind generator?

author: @ibme8359
text: I know, have you heard of a "couch potato"? Got a new one "video watcher specialist" ! Got to admit I do watch you and various others, but with notebook and pen in hand. Then I get up off my chair, head to my shop and try out various ideas. (Yes sometimes I need a few things, I visit local shops or go on line) and get started. As you say,  "Do it", yes sometimes I do find (failure is one of the options), but still lessons learned!!!

text: There's still some paywalls to get past, depending on the things you'd want to do. But then you need a certain income, and then figuring out to get qualifications and past the HR hiring game and all that. Sometimes the path to doing a thing is so bonkers that you almost feel like jumping off a cliff.

text: Electricity down since yesterday around 16:00. Got up at 5:00, made coffee on a mini rocket stove, connected my car to a small inverter, connected the wifi and and my laptop. Enjoying this video all the more.😀

author: @johnx9318
text:
Sometimes, it just has to be said!
I'm getting off my arse now, thanks.

author: @Sailormoon1v
text: Another way to put it, perhaps: Get the tools you need to dive down a particular rabbit hole of your interest, and dive away. Along the way you will surely encounter other tantalizing rabbit holes. Do not hesitate to acquire the necessary tools to dive into those too, and dive. Within 1-3 years you will have accumulated a fornidible tinkerer's workstation in a relatively inexpensive, piecemeal way.

author: @AndreaDingbatt
text: Best Advice on the interweb!! Thank you Robert!!

author: @NoahNobody
text: Could you tell me which 3D printer to buy. I feel like I am procrastinating about buying one.

author: @makingd.o.123
text: This is so true I turned 55 this September and I've finally decided to learn something I've wanted to do for years the problem was I really couldn't find anyone to teach me and no YouTube videos that were any help I started in my early 30's when my father was dieing, he was in his early 50's when I was born so to keep my mind off of it and my sanity I began to teach myself the basics it was very slow going but had proven to myself I could do it but would put it aside because life got in the way and I needed to make a living but thanks to COVID of all things I've started teaching myself again and what took months to accomplish now takes days and I just keep getting faster but this is what I've managed to teach myself just by doing you just have to take the first step and do https://youtu.be/BmtPdB1wmhU?si=I3-vtFkcV12F-knc

author: @davidblyth5495
text:
During an engineering seminar at work, a new graduate asked "When does the skills transfer start for us?"
My response "As soon as you start doing the projects assigned to you!".
We believed that competence is a combination of knowledge, skills and attitude.
The first two take time and depend on your attitude and effort.
Only I can make myself competent

author: @TheLidlClan
text: I have seen this video before and Love it, It's so true.

author: @matteonicoli
text: I would start with 3D drawing (blender, onshape), making a battery pack you can manage (maybe also rapsberry), everything else like wind energy is explained on this channel. You should always start with the hard stuff, then you know for sure you will finish it (nothing worse than buying tools, 3d printers and not finishing stuff). Earn it.

author: @LawrenceMarks27
text: When I first started following, I was frustrated in a similar way that it was always building knowledge towards a capstone of an overall project.  Over time though, once I started getting into it, it's been a lot of fun iterating through it from the ground up.  Definitely expands the core knowledge, which doesn't just allow us to do the action with greater chance of success, it also allows us to troubleshoot problems more easily because you understand the core of the system inside and out.

author: @Istandby666
text:
These kids are used to instant gratification. They don't actually understand it takes time to acquire the knowledge.
Do yourself a favor, put your cellphone away. Sit down and write an actual write a letter to someone. Mail it and wait for a response in the mail, it might take a few weeks.
But for the life within yourself. Turn that phone off and don't touch it for a week.

author: @R-Arand
text: "The shortest answer is doing the thing."  Ernest Hemingway

author: @Istandby666
text:
Most of the time i listen to what your saying but I'm thinking, that would work well if I add this to that.
Do I know the outcome? No. That's the whole point of doing the experiment.
Can I get a better understanding by doing all the math? Sure. But math is only going to get me so far. I'm still going to have to do the experiment. That's why it's called an experiment.
Don't be afraid to fail. Infact, I want my experiments to fail. By failing I understand what it can and can not do. It also teaches me what's wrong with my ideal.
The moral of the story is DON'T be afraid to fail. Failure is your friend not your enemy.

replies.author: @yasirrakhurrafat1142
replies.text:
Yeap!
An innovative idea comes to mind.
You act on it. Maybe it fails miserably.
Maybe you figure you can now build something never before made!
It is always more trials, and more info. Thus, better judgement.

author: @howardg396
text: Absolutely great advice. I've always one of those people who wanted to know as much as possible before starting a project, and this has slowed me down a fair bit in what I've accomplished. Keep up the good work!

replies.author: @pedjamilosavljevic6235
replies.text: There are two sides to that coin...Let's assume that you've gathered all the knowledge you need (including some practice) for the project , but what will you do if you can't gather materials and/or tools absolutely needed for it (because lack of financiers , or lack of availability of materials/tools/space to work in , or , circumstantially , illegality of project , or materials needed...). This advice is good for people that want to have some hobby (probably from well developed , industrialized and consume oriented countries and areas - most probably urban areas) and not so good for people that have a problem they need to solve (I mean , without throwing money at it , as in , purchasing all the necessary stuff/equipment , because lack of money/availability/opportunity... ). For an example , you can make some kind of windmill (turbine) to produce electricity extremely cheap , almost anywhere in the world (scrounging ,scrapyard , donations...) , but  , when comes to storage of energy - things get little more complicated , not to mention electronics (charger , inverter...) - there are places where you can't get ordinary used (and still useful) car battery , let alone other stuff.

author: @Istandby666
text:
A lot of the kids today (people under the age of 32) need everything handed to them. They can't think for themselves.
I'm more scared by the lack of knowledge these children have then I am a nuclear war.
The worst part, They think they're smart.
